claim_status: No-claim package/update source observations only; these sources are not a Turing updater, package-format selection, signing decision, rollback result, migration result, release approval, supported-security claim, or production evidence.

sources.title: Roles and metadata
sources.observation: TUF defines Root, Targets, Snapshot, and Timestamp roles; signed metadata carries expiration, thresholds, target hashes and sizes, and consistent repository-state checks.
sources.decision_consequence: Future update evidence must keep role authorization, threshold, expiry, target identity, size, channel, platform, architecture, and minimum-secure-version decisions separate; TUF does not select Turing's package, installer, profile, or channel policy.

sources.title: Security properties of TUF repositories
sources.observation: TUF documents arbitrary-installation, rollback, key-compromise, freeze, and related update-system attack classes and uses expiry and compartmentalized trust as design principles.
sources.decision_consequence: The lab must retain negative cases for stale or replayed metadata, rollback, compromised keys, arbitrary targets, and freeze-like unavailability; a valid signature alone cannot establish freshness or safe installation.

sources.title: SLSA v1.2 build provenance
sources.observation: SLSA provenance describes how an artifact was produced through build definition, resolved dependencies, builder identity, invocation metadata, and output subjects.
sources.decision_consequence: A future package record must distinguish source, build, dependency, builder, and artifact identity; provenance is production evidence and is not update authorization, installation success, key recovery, or profile migration safety.

sources.title: SLSA verifying artifacts
sources.observation: SLSA verification guidance requires consumers to interpret the provenance predicate and verify the artifact and provenance relationship rather than treating an attestation as an unbounded claim.
sources.decision_consequence: Future verification fixtures must bind the exact artifact, accepted predicate/version, builder identity, and policy; a provenance check must not be silently promoted into a release or supported-version decision.

sources.title: Getting started
sources.observation: in-toto uses an owner-signed layout and functionary-signed link metadata to describe authorized supply-chain steps and the materials and products of each step.
sources.decision_consequence: Future release evidence must separate authorized step identity, materials/products, layout expiry, and artifact signatures; in-toto does not decide Turing's update roles, installer transaction, or emergency authority.

sources.title: Cosign signing overview
sources.observation: Sigstore documents identity-bound signing workflows and verification material for software artifacts.
sources.decision_consequence: Signing experiments must record signer identity, artifact digest, verification policy, privacy impact, and recovery assumptions; Sigstore remains a candidate evidence model, not a selected Turing trust root or offline-key policy.

sources.title: Sigstore bundle format
sources.observation: Sigstore bundles can carry signatures, certificates, and transparency-log verification material together with an artifact reference.
sources.decision_consequence: A future packet must retain the bundle, artifact digest, verification configuration, transparency evidence, and redaction/access treatment; bundle presence is not proof of channel policy, rollback, migration, or release authority.

unsupported: No Turing package, updater, installer, metadata parser, signing hierarchy, or stable channel has been implemented or selected.
No fake-key update lab, installation fault rehearsal, rollback result, profile migration result, or owner-reviewed PB-017 readiness decision exists.
No production signing keys, offline roots, real updater service, public distribution, or real user profile is used by this manifest.
No release readiness, supported-security, rollback safety, migration safety, production, Chrome-class, performance, or broad implementation claim follows from these sources.
